lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying bulk surfactants, wetting agents, emulsifiers, penetrants, and textile or leather finishing agents to external industrial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive production, distributors without manufacturing operations, shells, non-control investments, and makers whose activities fall primarily in detergents, polishes, cosmetics, or unrelated chemical classes.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B product sales, often specification- and formulation-dependent, with revenue driven by volumes, product mix, raw-material pass-through, and customer qualification rather than hourly billing.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across the broader 3255/3256 chemical-manufacturing group, not wage-weighted tasks in 325613. O*NET describes the leading chemical-operator occupation but not AI exposure. I map recordkeeping, monitoring, scheduling, quality-data review, sales support, and administration to exposed work while treating physical handling, sampling, maintenance, and safety-critical control as largely unexposed; broader-industry aggregation may over- or understate the true 325613 mix.
a.rationale: The broader industry's largest occupations are predominantly plant-floor roles. Chemical operators perform both information-rich monitoring and physical, safety-sensitive tasks, so only a minority of wage-weighted current work is credibly substitutable by AI within five years.
a.change_evidence: A 325613 establishment-level payroll extract by occupation combined with structured task-time interviews for operators, chemists, quality staff, sales, planning, and administration.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix was located.
Exposure is task-level judgment, not measured adoption or realized headcount reduction.

rho.bridge: Census reports AI use for US businesses by size and NIST describes manufacturing use cases and implementation barriers, rather than five-year implementation of exposed work in 325613. I translate the observed diffusion and manufacturing constraints into an implementation share; small-plant legacy systems and regulated quality controls likely make the national and large-firm adoption evidence upward-biased for the LMM lens.
rho.rationale: Document processing, forecasting, exception detection, and compliance support can be deployed incrementally, but legacy integration, sparse labeled process data, cybersecurity, skills, validation, and human accountability constrain implementation on chemical lines.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level digital-maturity audits and vendor-verified before/after results for at least 15 LMM surfactant or specialty-chemical plants, including validated labor hours saved.
caveats: Census adoption covers any business function and all industries.
NIST use cases are manufacturing-wide and not controlled outcome studies.

e.rationale: Most operating firms classified here should repeatedly sell surfactants or finishing agents externally, but some estimated LMM-band firms may be captive plants, divisions, distributors with incidental production, or economically dependent operations that cannot transfer as standalone businesses.
e.change_evidence: A complete firm-level 325613 roster matched to ownership, standalone financial statements, customer invoices, and plant-control status.
caveats: The injected firm count is an estimate and may classify establishments rather than transferable standalone firms.
EPA's small-business threshold does not establish LMM eligibility.

s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability in the low twenties is plausible for privately held LMM manufacturers, reflecting succession, strategic sales, and sponsor activity, but public business-dynamics data track births, deaths, and age rather than qualifying control transfers at six-digit NAICS.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, or GF Data transaction records for US 325613 and adjacent surfactant producers, reconciled to a firm roster and supplemented by owner-age and succession interviews.
caveats: No direct six-digit transfer-rate series was found.
Shutdowns, internal reorganizations, minority investments, and plant asset sales are not qualifying transfers.

q.rationale: AI savings initially accrue to the manufacturer under product pricing, but industrial buyers can reclaim part through negotiated renewals, formula pricing, competitive bids, and qualification of alternate suppliers. Formulation know-how, service, and switching costs should preserve some benefit.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract review covering price-adjustment clauses, open-book costing, rebate structures, bid frequency, product qualification timelines, and gross-margin retention after historical process improvements.
caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Raw-material pass-through and customer concentration can dominate observed pricing independently of AI savings.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for total US manufacturing, not constant-quality demand for 325613. I use the sector's 1.3% 2024-34 annual output growth only as a broad anchor, tempering it for a mature intermediate-chemical category; this may miss faster substitution toward safer surfactants or weaker textile-linked demand.
d5.rationale: Surfactants are functional inputs across cleaning and industrial processes, supporting stable repeat volume. Mature end markets and formulation substitution limit upside, while environmental criteria can redirect rather than eliminate category demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of customer-level constant-price volume by application plus end-market forecasts for cleaning, personal care, agriculture, coatings, oilfield, textile, and leather uses.
caveats: The BLS projection is sector-wide, not six-digit.
Product-mix and quality changes are difficult to separate from volume in public data.

o.rationale: Year-five surfactant demand still requires accountable physical formulation, reaction, blending, testing, regulatory documentation, and delivery. Software can improve these activities but cannot supply the chemical product; limited customer self-manufacture or consolidation is the main displacement route.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and win/loss data quantifying insourcing, direct sourcing from large integrated chemical companies, and supplier consolidation by end market.
caveats: This is a bounded judgment rather than a measured displacement rate.
Large customers may vertically integrate or reformulate away from particular surfactants.

narrative.executive_view: This is a recurring B2B materials business with durable need for physical operators, but its modest compensation intensity limits the economic weight of labor automation. The most credible AI gains sit around the plant and laboratory workflow rather than in unattended process control.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist batch-record extraction, production planning, predictive maintenance, quality trending, regulatory document search, demand forecasting, and sales support. Sampling, material handling, equipment maintenance, emergency response, and release accountability remain human- and equipment-dependent.
narrative.value_capture: Product pricing permits initial retention of workflow savings, but concentrated industrial buyers, periodic rebids, alternate qualifications, and raw-material formula clauses can transfer part of the benefit through lower prices or slower increases.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied estimate of 16 LMM-band firms implies a small target universe. Many should be externally selling operating manufacturers, but corporate divisions and captive plants reduce eligibility, and no direct six-digit control-transfer series establishes availability.
narrative.demand_durability: Surfactants perform persistent physical functions such as wetting, emulsifying, and dispersing. Environmental and toxicity pressure should change chemistry and qualification requirements more than eliminate aggregate need, although textile and leather exposure and customer reformulation add downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest evidence gaps are six-digit occupation weights, realized AI labor savings, firm ownership structure, transaction frequency, contract pass-through, and application-level real volumes. Chemical safety, TSCA obligations, legacy controls, cyber risk, and customer qualification slow implementation and make autonomous recommendations risky.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat industrial demand for physical surfactant functions keeps the accountable manufacturer central to the customer workflow.
narrative.principal_weakness: The target pool is small and AI addresses only a minority of labor in a safety-critical, relatively low-labor-intensity manufacturing process.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 325613: Surface Active Agent Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the industry as bulk surface-active agents used as wetting agents, emulsifiers, and penetrants and textile/leather finishing agents.

sources.title: Largest Occupations in Chemical Manufacturing (3255 and 3256 only), May 2024
sources.supports: Reports 20,440 chemical equipment operators, 18,180 packaging/filling operators, 13,830 mixing/blending operators, and other leading occupations in the broader group.

sources.title: O*NET: Chemical Equipment Operators and Tenders
sources.supports: Lists operator tasks including recording operating data, controlling reactions, monitoring gauges, sampling and testing, inspecting equipment, handling ingredients, cleaning, and emergency response.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Census BTOS analysis reports overall business AI use around 17-20% in Dec. 2025-May 2026, 37% for firms with at least 250 employees, and 32% for firms with 100-249 employees.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing
sources.supports: Identifies manufacturing AI uses in predictive maintenance, quality control, and demand forecasting and barriers including data quality, initial cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration.

sources.title: Safer Choice Criteria for Surfactants
sources.supports: Shows that surfactant acceptability depends on aquatic toxicity, biodegradation, degradation products, and in some cases structural or test data.

sources.title: TSCA Fees and Small Businesses
sources.supports: Lists the TSCA small-business size standard for NAICS 325613 as 750 or fewer employees, confirming the code includes firms within a broad small-business regulatory category.

sources.title: About Business Dynamics Statistics
sources.supports: Explains that BDS measures establishment openings/closings, firm startups/shutdowns, job flows, and firm age and size, but does not measure qualifying control transfers.

sources.title: Output by Major Industry Sector, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects US manufacturing real output from $5,696.8 billion in 2024 to $6,486.2 billion in 2034, a 1.3% compound annual growth rate.
